name: Zhang Fei
description: Zhang Fei, also known as Yide, was a prominent general serving under the warlord Liu Bei during the late Eastern Han dynasty and the early Three Kingdoms period of China. Renowned for his unparalleled bravery, immense strength, and fiery temper, Zhang Fei was one of the Five Tiger Generals of Shu Han. Born in Zhuo Commandery, he joined Liu Bei and Guan Yu as sworn brothers, forming a formidable trio bound by loyalty and shared ambition. Throughout his military career, Zhang Fei participated in numerous battles, displaying exceptional martial prowess and often turning the tide of war with his ferocious charges and intimidating presence. His booming voice and imposing appearance were legendary, capable of striking fear into the hearts of his enemies. Zhang Fei's most famous feat was his stand at Changban Bridge, where he single-handedly deterred Cao Cao's pursuing army with his thunderous roar, allowing Liu Bei's forces and civilians to escape. Despite his valor, Zhang Fei's impulsive nature and excessive drinking often led to problems. He was eventually assassinated by his own subordinates, Fan Jiang and Zhang Da, while preparing for a campaign against the Kingdom of Wu. Zhang Fei remains a beloved and iconic figure in Chinese culture, celebrated for his courage, loyalty, and unwavering commitment to his sworn brothers. His legend continues to inspire awe and admiration, solidifying his place as one of the greatest warriors in Chinese history. He embodies the ideals of righteousness, bravery, and brotherhood, making him a timeless symbol of heroism.
subtitle: The Fierce General of Shu Han
achievements: Sworn brotherhood with Liu Bei and Guan Yu
Numerous victories in battles against Cao Cao
One of the Five Tiger Generals of Shu Han

keyMyths.title: The Oath of the Peach Garden
keyMyths.description: Zhang Fei, Liu Bei, and Guan Yu swore an oath of brotherhood in a peach garden, pledging loyalty and mutual support. This bond became the foundation of their shared ambition and shaped their destinies. The oath symbolizes unwavering loyalty and brotherhood.

keyMyths.title: Zhang Fei at Changban Bridge
keyMyths.description: Facing Cao Cao's pursuing army, Zhang Fei stood alone at Changban Bridge, roaring ferociously and deterring the enemy advance. His bravery allowed Liu Bei's forces and civilians to escape. This event highlights Zhang Fei's unparalleled courage and intimidation tactics.

keyMyths.title: Zhang Fei's Death
keyMyths.description: Due to his violent temper and drunken behavior, Zhang Fei was assassinated by his own subordinates, Fan Jiang and Zhang Da, while preparing for a campaign. This tragic end serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked impulsiveness. The assassination was retribution for Zhang Fei's abuse.

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
sources.description: The primary source for Zhang Fei's life and deeds, though often embellished with fictional elements.
sources.text: The primary source for Zhang Fei's life and deeds, though often embellished with fictional elements.

sources.author: Chen Shou
sources.description: A historical account providing factual information about Zhang Fei, though less detailed than the Romance.
sources.text: A historical account providing factual information about Zhang Fei, though less detailed than the Romance.

associations.name: Bravery
associations.significance: Zhang Fei's courage in battle was legendary, often intimidating entire armies.

associations.name: Serpent Spear
associations.significance: His signature weapon, symbolizing his ferocity and martial prowess.

associations.name: Tiger
associations.significance: Represents his raw power, aggression, and indomitable spirit in battle.

associations.name: Brotherhood
associations.significance: His bond with Liu Bei and Guan Yu was central to his identity and actions.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Reflects his fiery temper and passionate nature, both in battle and in life.

associations.name: General
associations.significance: Signifies his military leadership and strategic importance to Shu Han.

associations.name: Black
associations.significance: Often associated with his imposing appearance and intimidating presence on the battlefield.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are renowned for their unmatched combat skills and pivotal roles in major conflicts, but also possess notable flaws (Achilles' heel, Zhang Fei's temper).
Zhang Fei's battle rage mirrors the frenzied state of Berserkers, instilling fear in enemies.
Both are legendary warriors known for their exceptional strength, battle prowess, and distinctive weaponry (Serpent Spear/Gáe Bulg).

worshipPractices.name: Temple Worship
worshipPractices.description: Temples dedicated to Zhang Fei can be found throughout China, where he is venerated as a god of war and protector of righteousness. Offerings are made to seek his blessings for courage, victory, and justice.

cultural.modernLegacy: Zhang Fei remains a popular figure in Chinese culture, celebrated in opera, literature, and video games. His image embodies bravery, loyalty, and righteous anger, continuing to inspire generations.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Sworn Brotherhood
extendedContent.content: Born in Zhuo Commandery (modern-day Hebei), Zhang Fei's early life is shrouded in some mystery, though he is believed to have been a butcher or wine merchant. His path intersected with Liu Bei and Guan Yu during the turbulent years of the late Eastern Han dynasty. Their shared ambition to restore the Han dynasty and their mutual respect led them to swear an oath of brotherhood in a peach garden, solidifying a bond that would define their lives. This oath, emphasizing loyalty and mutual support, became a cornerstone of their alliance and a symbol of their commitment to a common cause. Zhang Fei quickly proved his worth as a fierce warrior, demonstrating unwavering loyalty to Liu Bei from the very beginning, participating in suppressing the Yellow Turban Rebellion.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Battles
extendedContent.content: Zhang Fei participated in numerous pivotal battles throughout the late Eastern Han and early Three Kingdoms periods. His most famous exploit is arguably his stand at Changban Bridge. Facing an overwhelming force led by Cao Cao, Zhang Fei single-handedly held off the enemy advance, roaring with such ferocity that he deterred Cao Cao's troops from crossing. This act of bravery bought crucial time for Liu Bei to escape. Another notable achievement was his capture of the general Yan Yan, a feat accomplished not through brute force alone, but through cunning strategy. These victories cemented his reputation as one of the most formidable warriors of his time. Zhang Fei's bravery was instrumental in securing territory for Liu Bei, contributing significantly to the establishment of the state of Shu Han.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Enduring Influence
extendedContent.content: Zhang Fei's legacy extends far beyond his military achievements. He is immortalized as a symbol of courage, loyalty, and righteous indignation in Chinese culture. His character has been romanticized in the historical novel *Romance of the Three Kingdoms*, further solidifying his place in popular imagination. Temples dedicated to Zhang Fei can be found throughout China, where he is worshipped as a deity. His image continues to appear in various forms of media, from opera and theater to video games and films, demonstrating his enduring appeal. Zhang Fei represents the ideal of the valiant warrior, a figure whose unwavering commitment to his brothers and his cause continues to inspire awe and admiration. He remains a potent symbol of the values of loyalty, bravery, and justice.

shortDescription: Zhang Fei was a fearsome general under Liu Bei, renowned for his unmatched bravery and fiery temper. One of the legendary Five Tiger Generals of Shu Han.
